items.q: Is the oxidation state of an atom the same as its actual charge?
items.correct: Not necessarily — oxidation state is an accounting tool assuming all bonds are fully ionic; real charge is usually smaller.
items.misconception: The oxidation state of an atom is exactly equal to its real ionic charge in every compound.
items.random_wrong: The oxidation state of an atom is always equal to the number of electrons it has.
items.why: Oxidation states are written with charge-like signs, so students treat them as actual charges, particularly in covalent compounds where this is misleading.

items.q: What is reduction in terms of electrons?
items.correct: Gain of electrons (the oxidation number is reduced).
items.misconception: Reduction means losing oxygen and oxidation means gaining oxygen — full stop.
items.random_wrong: Reduction means a substance becomes physically smaller in size.
items.why: Students learn the oxygen definition first (from rusting/combustion) and never update to the more general electron-transfer definition.

items.q: At dynamic equilibrium, what is happening to the reactions?
items.correct: Forward and reverse reactions continue at equal rates; concentrations remain constant.
items.misconception: Both reactions stop because the system has reached balance.
items.random_wrong: The forward reaction stops once the reverse reaction begins.
items.why: The word 'equilibrium' connotes stillness, so students think reactions stop — they miss that equilibrium is dynamic at the particle level.

items.q: Which of these is a greenhouse gas?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Carbon dioxide. “Nitrogen” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which activity adds the MOST CO₂ to the atmosphere?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Burning fossil fuels. “Planting trees” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Roughly what percentage of the atmosphere is currently CO₂ (in percent)?
items.why: The real figure is 0.04. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
